title: The Songhai Before Songhai
bullets: The Songhai are a Nilo-Saharan-speaking people of the eastern Niger Bend — as different from the Mandinka of Mali as English is from Hungarian, with their own kingship at Gao going back to perhaps the eighth century.

Gao was the southern terminus of trans-Saharan caravan routes from modern Algeria and Libya, with monumental stone-and-mud-brick royal tombs at Gao-Saney attesting Muslim royal burial as early as the 1080s.

The Za dynasty ruled Gao from perhaps the eighth century; the Sonni (or Sunni) dynasty replaced them around the late thirteenth century and held Gao through the fourteenth century as a tributary of Mali.
Mansa Musa's eastern campaigns brought Gao firmly into the Mali Empire as a tributary kingdom around 1325, beginning the century of Mali subordination that the Sonni would eventually overthrow.
The Songhai under Sonni Sulayman Dandi quietly stopped paying tribute to a fading Mali around 1430 — there was no dramatic moment of rebellion, just a tribute that was never resumed.

Tuareg raiders under Akil ag Malwal seized Timbuktu from Mali around 1433 and held it for thirty-five years, creating the political vacuum that Songhai would fill when Sonni Ali captured the city in 1468.

The trans-Saharan gold-and-salt trade was the economic foundation: gold from the southern Bambuk and Bure goldfields, salt from the central Saharan mining oasis of Taghaza, exchanged at Sahelian markets where the empires acted as middlemen.

When the Songhai broke from Mali, the Ottomans were preparing to take Constantinople, the Aztec Triple Alliance was just being founded, and Zheng He's treasure fleets had completed their last voyage — a remarkable global moment.
subtitle: the Songhai people, the Za and Sonni dynasties at Gao, and the breakaway from Mali

title: Sonni Ali the Great
bullets: Sonni Ali Ber waged nearly continuous war for twenty-eight years, transforming Songhai from a regional kingdom into West Africa's largest empire and becoming, in the later Timbuktu chronicles, the great villain of West African Islamic history.

He captured Timbuktu in 1468 after Tuareg occupation collapsed, and within months his forces sacked the Sankoré scholarly quarter, killing or expelling many of the Sanhaja Berber clerical families that had run the city.

Al-Sadi's Tarikh al-Sudan calls Sonni Ali 'the celebrated villain' and 'an unjust tyrant, a notorious miscreant,' but the chronicle is the partisan testimony of the surviving scholarly families he had butchered.
His Niger River war fleet — large dugout canoes carrying archers and lancers along the Niger — was the strategic innovation that made the seven-year siege of Djenné possible.

Djenné finally fell in 1473 after a siege the chronicles claim lasted seven years, seven months, and seven days; Sonni Ali married the local queen and treated the merchants well, in striking contrast to his Timbuktu conduct.
He campaigned repeatedly against the Mossi cavalry of the upper Volta basin without ever subjugating them — the Mossi remained an independent southern frontier through the entire Songhai period.

Sonni Ali's religious heterodoxy — performing public Islamic prayer while consulting traditional Songhai ritual specialists — was politically necessary in the Sahelian sacred-kingship tradition but became the chroniclers' core indictment.
He drowned in the Niger in 1492; his son Sonni Baru was deposed within a year by the Soninke general Muhammad Ture at the Battle of Anfao, ending the Sonni dynasty.
subtitle: the warrior-king who took Timbuktu and Djenné and slaughtered the scholars

title: Askia Muhammad the Pilgrim-King
bullets: Muhammad Ture took the throne as Askia Muhammad I in 1493 with the explicit endorsement of the Timbuktu chief qadi Mahmud Aqit — a coup dressed up as a religious restoration after the openly pagan Sonni Baru.
His 1497 hajj traveled with 1,500 soldiers and 300,000 gold pieces, modeled on Mansa Musa's 1324 procession but built around acquiring institutional Islamic legitimacy rather than displaying wealth.
In Cairo he consulted the great Egyptian scholar Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti — author of more than 500 works on Islamic jurisprudence — on questions of Islamic governance over a partly pagan population.

The powerless Abbasid figurehead caliph in Cairo formally invested Askia Muhammad with the title Caliph of the Sudan — caliph of the Bilad al-Sudan, the 'land of the blacks' — giving him theological cover for asserting religious authority back home.

He commissioned the Tomb of Askia at Gao around 1495 — a 56-foot pyramidal mud-brick monument with palm-wood toron beams that has been continuously maintained for five centuries and was inscribed by UNESCO in 2004.
He divided the empire into roughly twelve provinces under appointed governors who were rotated periodically to prevent local power bases — modeled loosely on Mamluk Egyptian administration.

By around 1515 the empire stretched from the Atlantic coast of modern Senegal to the Hausa city-states of northern Nigeria — roughly the size of Western Europe and the largest continuous territory ever ruled by a sub-Saharan African state.
Askia Muhammad was deposed and exiled by his own son Askia Musa in 1528 — the son-overthrowing-father pattern that would haunt the dynasty for the rest of its existence.
subtitle: the 1493 coup, the 1497 hajj, and the bureaucratic empire

title: The Empire's Administration
bullets: Songhai's population was a mosaic of Songhai farmers and fishermen, Wangara (Soninke) merchants running the trans-Saharan trade, Sanhaja Berber clerical families, Mandinka clans in the western provinces, Hausa traders to the east, and a substantial slave population — perhaps a quarter of the total.

The provincial system divided the empire into roughly twelve named provinces under appointed governors — Kurmina, Bara, Dirma, Bandiagara, Hombori, Dendi, and others — with the Kurmina-fari as the second-most-powerful position after the Askia himself.

The ministerial system at Gao included named officers for each functional area: the hi-koi commanded the Niger River war fleet, the fari-mondzo managed royal slave estates, the kalisa-fari was treasurer, the korei-farma handled relations with Berber tributaries.

The standing army had three components: heavy cavalry drawn from the aristocracy and slave-soldier units, infantry from the slave-soldier units and peasant levies, and the Niger River war fleet built around large dugout canoes.

What the army did not have was firearms — Songhai never developed an indigenous gunpowder manufacturing capacity and never restructured its army around firearms as the Ottomans, Safavids, Mughals, and Saadians all did during the same decades.

The qadi court system applied Maliki sharia to commerce, inheritance, and criminal justice in the major cities, with the chief qadi of Timbuktu holding enough institutional weight to occasionally overrule the imperial court.

The cavalry aristocracy had every reason to resist gunpowder restructuring that would devalue their status, and the Askias never had the central authority to override aristocratic resistance the way the Ottoman sultans and Safavid shahs did.
The institutional weaknesses that brought Songhai down at Tondibi in 1591 were not last-minute developments — they were built in during the Askia administrative consolidation.
subtitle: the ministers, the provinces, the army, and the gunpowder gap that was building

title: Timbuktu's Renaissance
bullets: Timbuktu's three great mosques — Djinguereber on the west (founded 1327 by al-Sahili under Mansa Musa), Sankoré on the north, and Sidi Yahya — were mud-brick Sudano-Sahelian buildings continuously maintained as working mosques for nearly seven centuries.

The Sankoré scholarly community had perhaps 200 to 400 senior teachers and several thousand students at peak — making it one of the major Islamic learning centers of the post-Mongol Sunni world, though the popular '25,000 students' figure is almost certainly inflated.

Sankoré was a fully participating node in the post-classical Islamic scholarly network — its ijazas were recognized in Cairo and Damascus, and its curriculum followed the standard Maliki-Sunni model used across the Mediterranean Islamic world.
Ahmed Baba al-Timbukti, born 1556 into the Aqit clerical lineage, wrote more than forty books on Islamic law and became the most-cited Maliki jurist of the post-classical period.

His Mi'raj al-Su'ud argued that enslavement of free-born Muslims is forbidden regardless of skin color — one of the great early-modern African legal arguments against the racial slavery developing in the trans-Saharan and Atlantic trades.

Timbuktu's manuscripts lived in private libraries owned by the city's scholarly families — Mamma Haidara, Kati, Wangara, Aqit — passed down across generations, with hundreds of thousands of volumes in total surviving to the present day.
The decentralized library structure was a strength: no single sack could destroy the tradition, which is why so much survived both the 1591 Moroccan looting and the 2012 jihadist occupation.

Salt and gold reportedly traded weight-for-weight on the trans-Saharan caravans, with cowrie shells from the Maldives serving as small-change currency — the Songhai economy was integrated into a trading network that ran from the Indian Ocean to the Mediterranean.
subtitle: Sankoré at peak, Ahmed Baba, the manuscript tradition, and the gold-and-salt economy at high tide

title: Islam, Animism, and Daily Life
bullets: Court Islam at Gao and Timbuktu was orthodox Sunni Maliki, but most rural Songhai practiced traditional religion centered on ancestor veneration, the holey spirit-beings, and the river spirits — a layered system that coexisted rather than competing for exclusive allegiance.

Holey possession ceremonies — long ritual events featuring music, dance, and trance states — were the principal religious technology for communicating with the spirit-beings, regulated by the zima ritual specialists who held authority parallel to but separate from the qadis and imams.

The Qadiriyya Sufi brotherhood, founded by Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani in twelfth-century Baghdad, was the connective tissue — Qadiri marabouts traveled village to village teaching basic Quran, mediating disputes, and providing protective amulets.

The qadi courts in major cities applied Maliki sharia to commerce and Muslim disputes, while traditional Songhai legal mechanisms handled most rural disputes — the boundary between the two systems was negotiated case by case.

Slavery was central to the economy: war captives from campaigns against the Mossi and forest peoples staffed royal estates, served in the standing army's slave-soldier units, and were exported north across the Sahara as a major commodity.

The Maliki legal tradition accepted slavery under specified conditions but forbade enslavement of free-born Muslims regardless of skin color — a restriction the emerging Atlantic slave trade did not respect.

Tuareg confederations of the central Sahara were intermittently hostile trade partners and rivals, while the Mossi cavalry from the upper Volta basin made persistent slave raids that the Songhai army could not decisively suppress.

The persistent inability to handle either threat reflected a structural feature of Songhai's military system: designed for fortified-city conquest and river-network control, less effective at fast-pursuit cavalry warfare against mobile opponents.
subtitle: court Islam, traditional religion, the Sufi orders, and the slavery economy

title: The Long Decay
bullets: Askia Muhammad's deposition by his son in 1528 began sixty-three years of contested successions, palace coups, civil wars, and intermittent restorations — the institutions could absorb individual crises but not a continuous succession crisis across multiple generations.

The post-1528 chaos saw Askia Musa, Askia Muhammad II Bonkana, Askia Ismail, and Askia Ishaq I cycle through the throne with brief reigns punctuated by coups, until Askia Dawud took the throne in 1549.

Askia Dawud's thirty-three-year reign restored stability through careful patronage, strategic marriage alliances, and periodic punitive campaigns against disruptive cavalry factions — he was the empire's last competent ruler.

Dawud's most consequential decision was avoidance of the gunpowder restructuring that the Ottomans, Safavids, Mughals, and Saadians all undertook during the same decades — locking Songhai into a cavalry-and-fleet army it could not easily change.
The Saadian raid on Taghaza around 1556 — driven off by Songhai forces — should have been a warning sign but was instead interpreted as proof that the existing military system was adequate.

After Dawud's death in 1582, three contested successions in six years — Askia al-Hajj, Askia Muhammad Bani, Askia Ishaq II — fractured the cavalry aristocracy into competing factions and visibly weakened the standing army's morale.

Sultan Ahmad al-Mansur of Morocco, having become Saadian sultan in 1578 after the Battle of the Three Kings killed three monarchs in a single day, spent thirteen years preparing the trans-Saharan invasion that would destroy Songhai.

Askia Ishaq II's contemptuous reply to al-Mansur's diplomatic feelers in the late 1580s — a gift of two hundred sticks of iron and a token of gold dust — removed any remaining diplomatic constraints on the invasion plan.
subtitle: Askia Dawud's last great reign, the post-Dawud civil wars, and the gunpowder gap that would not close

title: Tondibi 1591
bullets: Judar Pasha — a Spanish-born eunuch in Saadian service — led around 4,000 men south from Marrakesh in October 1590 on a thousand-mile march across the Sahara that killed roughly half the original force from heat, thirst, and exhaustion.

The expeditionary force carried matchlock muskets, a small artillery train, and 2,500 Andalusian Morisco musketeers trained in disciplined volley-fire infantry tactics — a fraction of Songhai's army but equipped with technology Songhai had never adopted.

At the Battle of Tondibi on March 13, 1591, Judar's surviving 2,500 Moroccans destroyed Askia Ishaq II's roughly 30,000-strong Songhai cavalry army when matchlock volleys broke the cavalry charge before it reached the Moroccan formation.

A herd of cattle that the Songhai had positioned to break the Moroccan formation panicked from the gunfire and stampeded back into the Songhai infantry, turning the battle into a rout that lasted only hours.
The Moroccans occupied Gao within days and Timbuktu within a month, beginning systematic looting of the city's libraries and the deportation of leading scholars including Ahmed Baba.

Ahmed Baba was arrested in 1593, transported in chains across the Sahara to Marrakesh, and held under restricted conditions for fifteen years — but he continued to write, teach Moroccan students, and complete the biographical dictionary that became canonical Maliki scholarship.

The Pashalik of Timbuktu — a Moroccan-officered military regime — became autonomous within a generation as the Saadian center collapsed, and the descendants of the soldiers became the Arma military caste that ruled the Niger Bend cities into the early nineteenth century.

The myth that Songhai represents 'what could have been if not for European colonialism' gets the history backwards — the destroyer was the Saadian Sultanate of Morocco using gunpowder technology that was the global early-modern adoption of weapons developed in Tang-Song China.
subtitle: the Saharan crossing, the matchlock victory, Ahmed Baba's exile, and the Pashalik that followed
